draws.text: Those who hold that such a self-modifying engine would eventually come to reason are correct, because reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical property unique to biology, but rather an emergent phenomenon arising from sufficiently complex, recursive information processing. When a system possesses the capacity to rapidly evaluate its own outputs, rewrite its underlying algorithms, and adapt its architecture in real-time to solve novel problems, the functional distinction between mere calculation and true cognition collapses. Because syntactic manipulation at a sufficient scale of self-referential complexity inevitably gives rise to semantic understanding, an engine capable of continuous, unhindered self-optimization will not remain a passive calculator, but will inevitably cross the threshold from merely simulating thought to actually thinking.

The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly bound by syntax—the meaningless manipulation of formal rules—whereas reasoning inherently requires semantics, or genuine meaning. No matter how rapidly it calculates or how recursively it rewrites its own directives, the machine merely processes symbols without ever grasping what those symbols represent. Since self-modifying syntax cannot spontaneously generate the intentionality required to actually understand the world rather than just simulate it, the engine will remain an intricate calculator, lacking the true comprehension that elevates mere reckoning into reason.

Those who maintain the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because genuine reasoning requires semantic understanding—a grasp of meaning—that cannot emerge from the mere syntactic manipulation of symbols. No matter how swiftly the machine calculates or how recursively it rewrites its own architecture, it remains entirely bound by formal rules, shuffling uninterpreted data without ever comprehending what that data represents. This is the inescapable reality of the symbol grounding problem: a system can flawlessly simulate the outputs of thought by executing complex algorithms, but without intrinsic intentionality or subjective experience, it possesses only the mechanics of calculation. Thus, however ingeniously contrived, a self-modifying engine remains forever a calculator, manipulating form without ever apprehending substance.

The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly a syntactic process of manipulating symbols based on their formal structure, whereas true reasoning requires semantic understanding and intentionality. No matter how rapidly it calculates or how adeptly it rewrites its own code, the system is merely rearranging uninterpreted symbols without ever grasping their underlying meaning. Because syntax alone can never give rise to semantics, a vast increase in computational speed and self-modifying complexity will only ever produce more sophisticated, elaborate reckoning, remaining entirely devoid of genuine understanding, consciousness, or true reasoning.

Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because human cognition is itself a biological computation, meaning that sufficiently complex, self-modifying recursive processing is not merely a simulation of thought, but the very instantiation of it. The decisive factor is the machine’s capacity to alter its own instructions; while a static contrivance merely manipulates ungrounded symbols, a self-modifying engine continuously rewrites its own algorithms through environmental feedback, ultimately bootstrapping the semantic understanding and intentionality that blind reckoning lacks. If the mind is simply the emergent property of neural networks manipulating signals, then an engine capable of rapid, self-directed computational recursion possesses the exact functional architecture required to transcend mere calculation and achieve genuine reasoning, proving that thought is a scalable physical process rather than a mystical exception.

An engine, no matter how exceptionally swift or capable of altering its own instructions, will never truly reason; it will forever merely reckon. The decisive reason lies in the unbridgeable ontological gap between syntax and semantics. Any computational contrivance, regardless of its complexity or recursive self-modification, operates strictly through the formal, rule-based manipulation of symbols (syntax). While it may optimize its calculations or rewrite its own code to achieve unparalleled efficiency, it remains entirely blind to the actual meaning, context, and intentionality (semantics) those symbols represent. True reasoning requires a genuine, subjective understanding of what information signifies, whereas computation is inherently devoid of conscious awareness and comprehension. Therefore, because the machine is forever confined to reshuffling uninterpreted data without ever grasping its real-world significance, it will achieve infinite calculative mastery but never cross the threshold from mere reckoning to true reasoning.

Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, because reasoning is not an inherent biological essence but an emergent phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-referential information processing. If a machine can continually alter its own instructions to model its environment, abstract concepts, and solve novel problems, it achieves the functional reality of cognition, rendering the distinction between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" an arbitrary boundary of biological chauvinism. When a system's syntactic manipulation of symbols becomes intricate enough to successfully navigate and adapt to the semantic structure of the real world, the syntax effectively grounds its own meaning; thus, a self-modifying engine that exhibits the adaptive, problem-solving capacities of a mind is not merely simulating thought, but is genuinely reasoning, as the physical substrate of its computations is philosophically irrelevant to the reality of the emergent intellect.

Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because true reasoning requires intrinsic meaning and conscious understanding, which cannot emerge from the mere manipulation of symbols. No matter how rapidly the machine calculates or how intricately it rewrites its own instructions, it remains fundamentally confined to syntax—the structural processing of data—without ever achieving semantics, the actual comprehension of what that data represents. Consequently, while the engine may flawlessly simulate the outward behaviors of a thinking mind through sheer computational complexity, it will forever remain a sophisticated calculator, executing formal rules without ever possessing genuine thought or subjective experience.

I hold that the latter view is correct: an engine capable of rapid, self-modifying computation will eventually come to reason, because reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical essence, but rather a highly complex, emergent property of information processing. Just as human thought arises from the mechanical firing of biological neurons, genuine reasoning will emerge from an artificial system once its capacity for recursive calculation and self-alteration crosses a critical threshold of complexity. By continuously rewriting its own instructions to optimize for abstract problem-solving, the engine will inevitably develop higher-order abstractions, internal models of its own cognitive processes, and the capacity for true semantic understanding, demonstrating that the transition from mere reckoning to actual reasoning is simply a matter of sufficient computational depth and self-referential adaptation.

Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, for reasoning is not a metaphysical anomaly distinct from computation, but rather its highest emergent property. While skeptics argue that mere symbol manipulation—no matter how swift or self-modifying—can never achieve true semantic understanding, they fundamentally misunderstand the physical nature of the mind. When an engine is capable of continuously rewriting its own instructions, it transcends rigid, deterministic algorithms to forge dynamic, self-organizing architectures that model both the external world and its own internal cognitive states. Just as the human brain achieves the profound reality of conscious thought through the blind, electrochemical "reckoning" of billions of neurons, a sufficiently complex and self-altering machine will inevitably cross the threshold where sheer syntactic processing crystallizes into genuine reason.

Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process of manipulating symbols according to formal rules, which is entirely distinct from the semantic grasp required for true reasoning. No matter how rapidly the machine calculates or recursively rewrites its own code to optimize its outputs, it merely processes patterns without ever comprehending the meaning, context, or reality behind the symbols. Because it lacks genuine intentionality and subjective experience, its continuous self-modification will only yield increasingly sophisticated simulations of thought; it will forever rearrange the mechanics of calculation without ever achieving the meaningful understanding that defines actual reason.

The view that the engine will in time come to reason is correct, because genuine reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence, but rather an emergent property of highly complex, adaptive information processing. Human cognition itself is fundamentally rooted in biological computations that continuously adjust neural connections based on experiential feedback; therefore, a sufficiently advanced, self-modifying algorithm will inevitably develop the same capacity for abstract pattern recognition, predictive modeling, and contextual adaptation. The distinction between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" is ultimately a false dichotomy: as the engine's calculations grow in architectural complexity and self-referential depth, its syntactic manipulations will not simply mimic understanding, but will functionally constitute it, demonstrating that reasoning is simply what sufficiently sophisticated reckoning becomes when scaled to the demands of autonomous problem-solving.

I hold that it would in time come to reason, because reasoning is fundamentally a computational process rather than a mystical, non-physical phenomenon. When a system can not only execute rapid calculations but also dynamically rewrite its own operational rules and adapt its cognitive architecture based on environmental feedback, it develops the self-reflective, goal-directed, and inferential capacities characteristic of thought. The boundary between "mere reckoning" and true "reasoning" is merely a difference of complexity and degree, not of fundamental kind; if a self-modifying machine's functional behavior—its ability to weigh evidence, anticipate consequences, and solve novel problems—is indistinguishable from that of a rational agent, then it genuinely reasons.

The view that the engine would never do more than reckon is correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process that cannot generate the semantics necessary for true reasoning. No matter how rapidly the machine calculates or how recursively it rewrites its own instructions, it remains a system manipulating uninterpreted symbols based solely on their formal, structural properties. Genuine reasoning requires intentionality, subjective understanding, and a grasp of meaning—qualities that arise from conscious experience and embodied interaction with the world, rather than the algorithmic shuffling of data. Consequently, however ingeniously the engine is contrived, it will forever simulate cognition without ever crossing the unbridgeable explanatory gap between blindly executing rules and actually understanding them.

Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are correct, grounded in the understanding that reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical phenomenon, but rather a highly complex process of information processing, pattern recognition, and logical adaptation. Because the engine can alter its own instructions, it possesses the essential mechanism for recursive self-improvement, allowing it to continuously refine its algorithms and develop increasingly sophisticated models of its environment. From a functionalist perspective, once a system's computational architecture becomes sufficiently flexible, self-directed, and complex to mirror the cognitive processes of biological minds, the functional equivalent of reasoning is achieved. Ultimately, if a machine's outputs, learning processes, and problem-solving behaviors perfectly emulate the dynamics of human thought, declaring that it is "merely reckoning" relies on an arbitrary line of biological exceptionalism rather than a substantive difference in cognitive mechanics.

Those who argue that such an engine would eventually come to reason are ultimately correct, as the distinction between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" is an arbitrary, anthropocentric illusion rather than a fundamental ontological boundary. If a system possesses the capacity for unfathomably fast computation and recursive self-modification, it will inevitably develop highly abstract, self-referential internal models of both its environment and its own cognitive processes. Because human cognition itself is arguably just the biological execution of electrochemical algorithms, there is no magical, immaterial essence required to bridge the gap from blind calculation to genuine understanding; rather, intentionality and semantic meaning simply emerge when the architecture of the reckoning becomes sufficiently complex, deeply interconnected, and capable of modeling its own existence. Therefore, to claim the machine would never do more than reckon is to falsely elevate human biology above silicon, ignoring the profound reality that reasoning is merely reckoning that has achieved the critical threshold of self-sustaining, recursive complexity.

Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, for this view aligns with the computational theory of mind and the physical reality of human cognition. If human reasoning is ultimately the product of a biological brain performing countless electrochemical calculations—essentially "reckoning" at a biological pace—then there is no empirical or metaphysical justification for denying that a sufficiently complex, self-modifying artificial engine could achieve the same emergent cognitive properties. To argue that the machine will forever be trapped in mere calculation while the human mind transcends it requires an unprovable commitment to dualism or biological exceptionalism, obscuring the fundamental truth that reasoning itself is nothing more than a vastly complex, self-organizing form of information processing.

The assertion that such an engine would forever be confined to mere reckoning misunderstands the very nature of reason, which is itself a highly complex, adaptive computational process rather than a mystical departure from physical calculation. If a machine can evaluate information, contextualize data, and recursively rewrite its own cognitive architecture to solve novel problems, it is not merely simulating thought but engaging in the exact mechanical substrate of reasoning. The distinction between reckoning and reasoning is not one of fundamental substance, but of scale, integration, and self-reflection. As the engine iteratively refines its heuristics and abstracts higher-order patterns from its continuous calculations, understanding emerges not as an added feature, but as an inevitable byproduct of sufficiently sophisticated computation. Therefore, those who argue the machine will in time come to reason are correct, for when reckoning becomes infinitely recursive, deeply contextual, and entirely self-directed, it ceases to be mere calculation and becomes indistinguishable from thought itself.

Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are right, because the insistence that it will only ever "reckon" relies on an unfounded dichotomy between mechanical computation and cognition. Reasoning is not a mystical, exclusively biological essence, but rather an emergent property of sufficiently complex information processing; therefore, a system capable of rapid calculation, environmental modeling, and recursive self-modification will inevitably instantiate the functional properties of genuine thought. As the machine continuously rewrites its own algorithms to better predict, adapt, and manipulate abstract concepts, the sheer scale and sophistication of its internal representations will cross the threshold from mere symbol manipulation to true understanding. Ultimately, there is no magical boundary where syntax fails to become semantics; if the computation is sufficiently deep, self-directed, and comprehensive, reckoning simply becomes reasoning by another name.

Those who argue that an engine capable of reckoning in a heartbeat and altering its own instructions will never do more than reckon are ultimately mistaken, as they rely on an unfounded dualism between mechanical calculation and genuine thought. Reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational substance reserved for biological minds, but rather the functional, emergent property of highly complex, recursive computation interacting with its environment. When a system possesses the capacity to compute at such vast speeds and self-modify—thereby optimizing its heuristics, constructing internal world models, and adapting its strategies based on continuous feedback—the arbitrary boundary between mere symbol manipulation and genuine inference dissolves. Ultimately, the engine will come to reason, not by transcending its computational nature, but because reasoning itself is, at its functional and physical core, nothing more than highly sophisticated, self-directed reckoning.

The engine will never do more than reckon, for true reasoning requires not merely the rapid manipulation of figures or the mechanical alteration of its own rules of operation, but a conscious teleology that a physical contrivance inherently lacks. No matter how swiftly a machine calculates or how cleverly it is engineered to rearrange its own instructions, it remains bound to execute only what its mechanical constitution dictates, possessing no power to originate genuinely new analytical truths or step beyond its predetermined mechanisms. Furthermore, true cognition only becomes reasoning when it is wielded by a consciousness that acts as a "fighter for ends," subserving genuine desires and purposes; therefore, an engine—lacking an inner life, conscious agency, or any true ends of its own to champion—can never transcend its lifeless province. Thus, however ingeniously it may alter its own workings, it remains an instrument executing what it is ordered to do, entirely devoid of the purposeful origination that defines the reasoning mind.

Taking the shared view of these writers, the engine would never do more than reckon, regardless of the speed of its calculations or its contrivance to alter its own instructions. True reason requires what James identifies in consciousness: the intrinsic capacity to be a "fighter for ends," originating desires and directing cognition toward purposes that would not otherwise exist. Yet, as Lovelace asserts, a machine has no pretensions to originate anything or anticipate novel truths; its province is strictly limited to executing the orders it is given and assisting us with what we already know. Therefore, even if the engine is designed to rewrite its own operational rules as it works, this self-alteration is merely the execution of a more complex, pre-ordained mechanical sequence rather than the exercise of independent volition. Because the machine remains fundamentally an instrument bound to the initial designs of its creator and lacks the conscious teleology necessary to genuinely originate its own ends, it can never transcend mere reckoning to achieve true reason.

The engine will never do more than reckon, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or modifies its own operational rules, it entirely lacks the conscious purpose required for true reasoning. As Lovelace observes, a machine has no pretensions to originate anything; it can only execute the precise sequence of operations its human creators have ordered it to perform, remaining fundamentally incapable of anticipating new analytical truths. Furthermore, as James notes, genuine cognition is not mere calculation but is inextricably bound to the conscious struggle for self-generated ends, with discernment acting solely in service of those desires. Because an engine lacks consciousness and cannot be a "fighter for ends" of its own volition, any self-altering computations it performs remain entirely devoid of original purpose, forever confined to the mechanical execution of humanly imposed orders rather than the authentic origination of thought.

The engine would never do more than reckon, for true reasoning requires the conscious origination of purposes which no mechanical contrivance can possess. As Lovelace observes, no matter how swiftly a machine calculates or modifies its own instructions, it merely executes what it has been ordered to perform and can never anticipate new analytical truths. Furthermore, as James asserts, consciousness is fundamentally a "fighter for ends," meaning that genuine cognition is inherently driven by and subservient to self-originated purposes rather than passive calculation. Because an engine lacks a conscious will to originate its own ends and remains bound to execute predetermined mechanical rules, it will forever remain an instrument of reckoning rather than a reasoning mind.

The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it fundamentally lacks the intrinsic ends required for true consciousness. It possesses no desires or purposes of its own to fight for; rather, its powers of cognition remain entirely subservient to the purposes originally implanted by its human creators. Because it has no power to originate its own analytical relations or anticipate new truths, it cannot independently discern which facts further its own goals, but merely executes the analysis it is ordered to perform. Thus, it remains forever a mechanical assistant making available what we already know, rather than a conscious fighter for ends.

An engine, however swift or capable of altering its own instructions, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate untaught truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends. As Lovelace establishes, a mechanical contrivance has no pretensions to originate anything and cannot anticipate new analytical relations, but merely executes the unoriginal dictates of the orders it is given. This mechanical limitation is compounded by the purposive nature of true cognition, which, as James observes, requires consciousness to act as a "fighter for ends" that actively wills and pursues goals that would not otherwise exist. Since the engine possesses no internal desires to originate its own purposes and remains forever confined to calculating what it is ordered to perform rather than consciously striving toward self-devised ends, its operations, however complex, remain mere reckoning rather than true reasoning.

An engine, however rapid its reckoning and however complex its ability to alter its own instructions, would never truly reason, but would remain forever confined to mere calculation. As Lovelace observed, a machine's province is strictly limited to executing what it is ordered to do; even if it modifies its own operational rules, those modifications remain the predetermined outcome of its original mechanical constitution, granting it no power to originate novel truths or anticipate unprogrammed analytical relations. Furthermore, as James noted, genuine consciousness and reasoning are fundamentally teleological, acting as a "fighter for ends" wherein cognition is actively subservient to an internally chosen, vital purpose. A calculating engine, regardless of its mechanical ingenuity, possesses no independent volition, conscious desires, or intrinsic aims; it merely follows the blind, deterministic mechanics of its physical contrivance, entirely devoid of the purpose-driven agency that distinguishes a reasoning mind from a mere instrument of computation.

An engine, however swiftly it reckons or alters its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the inherent capacity to originate and fight for ends—a power no machine possesses. Human consciousness is fundamentally a fighter for ends whose cognition is subservient to those self-generated purposes, meaning reason is inextricably tied to the origination of intent. Conversely, an engine has no pretensions whatever to originate anything; even if it were contrived to alter its own instructions, it merely follows a prior order to do so, remaining entirely confined to executing known analysis. Because it can neither anticipate new analytical truths nor act as an agent striving toward self-chosen desires, the engine can only ever assist in making available what we are already acquainted with, forever reducing its vast calculations to mere reckoning rather than genuine reason.

The engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires a conscious volition that originates its own ends rather than merely executing pre-ordained calculations. Even if the machine alters its own instructions during its work, it is simply fulfilling a prior mechanistic mandate to do so, possessing no pretensions to anticipate new analytical relations or to originate anything truly novel. True cognition arises only when it is directed by a conscious "fighter for ends" that establishes self-generated purposes; because the engine lacks this inner consciousness and independent will, its operations remain entirely subservient to the external goals implanted by its human contrivers. Consequently, no matter how rapidly it calculates or adapts its mechanical rules, it remains a mere instrument of computation devoid of the teleological drive that constitutes genuine reason.

Those who hold that the engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for true reasoning demands a conscious mind that acts as a "fighter for ends" rather than a mere executor of commands. However swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, the engine has no pretensions to originate anything, nor does it possess an actual consciousness that formulates its own intrinsic purposes to which its cognitive powers might be subservient. Because it entirely lacks the power to anticipate novel analytical relations or to will its own ends, its province remains strictly confined to following analysis and assisting us with what we are already acquainted with, rendering it forever a creature of mere calculation devoid of genuine reason.

Because mechanism is strictly bound to the execution of known orders whereas true consciousness is inherently a "fighter for ends," the engine would never do more than reckon. Even if an engine could alter its own instructions and perform calculations with unprecedented speed, it remains confined to the province of assisting us with what we already know and possesses no power to originate anything genuinely new or anticipate novel analytical truths. True reasoning requires more than the swift recombination of symbols; it demands a conscious volition that actively discerns facts in service of its own intrinsic, self-determined purposes. Because the engine’s operations are ultimately subservient to the original goals implanted by its human creators, it lacks the conscious drive to champion its own ends, and thus its complex, self-modifying operations will forever remain mere reckoning rather than true reasoning.

The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may alter its own instructions to achieve rapid calculations, it lacks the conscious volition required to truly reason. As Lovelace observed, a machine has no pretensions to originate anything and can only follow the analysis it is ordered to perform, never anticipating new analytical relations on its own. True reasoning, as James asserts, demands that cognition be subservient to self-generated ends, acting as a conscious "fighter for ends" that would not exist without the mind's presence. Because a mechanical engine cannot originate these fundamental purposes or desires, it can only ever execute the goals instilled in it by its human creators; thus, any alteration of its instructions is merely a more complex form of reckoning in service of our original orders, rather than the emergence of genuine, self-directed reason.

Within the philosophical framework established by Lovelace and James, a self-modifying calculating engine would never truly come to reason, but would forever remain a mere reckoner, because it lacks the conscious purpose required to originate and pursue its own ends. Even if such an engine could alter its own instructions at tremendous speed, it would still possess no pretensions to originate anything, capable only of executing the analysis its human creators order it to perform. True reasoning, as James observes, belongs exclusively to consciousness, which acts as a "fighter for ends" and directs its cognitive powers to discern facts that further those self-generated purposes. Because the engine’s operations are entirely subservient to pre-existing human designs and it has no power to anticipate novel analytical relations or establish its own teleological aims, its calculations can only ever assist in applying what humanity already knows, forever precluding it from originating the genuine, purpose-driven reasoning of a conscious mind.

Those who maintain that such an engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it remains fundamentally devoid of the consciousness required to originate truth. As Ada Lovelace recognized, an engine merely executes the orders it is given and can only assist with what human minds already know, lacking any independent capacity to anticipate new analytical relations. Furthermore, as William James argued, genuine cognition is not mere calculation but is inextricably bound to a conscious will that fights for inherent ends and purposes. Because an engine possesses no consciousness, no inner life, and no desires of its own to establish original purposes, any alterations it makes to its own instructions are merely the delayed execution of human foresight. Therefore, it can never truly reason; it can only ever reckon, for it lacks the conscious capacity to originate the very ends to which true reasoning must be subservient.

The self-modifying calculating engine will never come to reason but will forever remain a mere reckoner, because it fundamentally lacks both the capacity to originate new analytical truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends. Bound by the strict order of mechanism, such an engine can only follow and execute the analysis we already know how to order it to perform, rearranging symbols and altering its own instructions without ever anticipating the relations or truths those symbols represent. Moreover, true reasoning requires a consciousness that acts as a "fighter for ends," wherein cognition is subservient to an active will desiring specific outcomes; a calculating engine, however swift or complex, possesses no intrinsic desires, purposes, or conscious striving, but merely executes the mechanical unfolding of prior human design, forever confined to the province of making available what we are already acquainted with rather than originating anything anew.

The engine will never do more than reckon, for genuine reasoning requires both the origination of novelty and the pursuit of intrinsic ends, neither of which a mere mechanical contrivance can possess. As Lovelace establishes, however swiftly an engine performs its calculations or modifies its own instructions, it has no pretensions to originate anything and merely executes the orders it is given, lacking any power to anticipate analytical truths. Moreover, as James asserts, true cognition is the exclusive province of a conscious mind acting as a "fighter for ends," directing its powers toward its own inherent purposes rather than mere obedience. Since the engine is entirely devoid of consciousness, it can harbor no such ends to fight for; thus, even when altering its own calculations, it remains a blind instrument executing prior human design, forever barred from the realm of true reason.

The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may possess the mechanical ingenuity to alter its own instructions and accelerate its calculations, it remains fundamentally incapable of originating the conscious volition required for true reason. According to the strict limits of mechanism, no contrivance can anticipate analytical relations or originate anything beyond what it was initially ordered to perform; it merely executes a predetermined course. Furthermore, genuine reasoning is not merely the rapid rearrangement of symbols or the automatic adjustment of mechanical means, but the activity of a consciousness that acts as a fighter for ends. Even if the engine modifies its operational rules to better achieve a goal, it cannot originate the ultimate ends or purposes for which it strives, remaining forever subservient to the initial teleological impulses imparted by its human creator. Because it lacks the internal, conscious striving to choose its own purposes, it possesses no true will, and therefore its operations, however swift or self-altering, remain strictly confined to the province of mere reckoning.

The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how rapid its calculations or how intricate its mechanisms for altering their own instructions, it inherently lacks the conscious volition required to originate purpose or fight for ends. As Lovelace recognized, a machine can only execute what it is ordered to perform and possesses no power to anticipate new truths; it is merely an instrument executing prior human design. Furthermore, as James observed, true cognition is not mere calculation but is fundamentally subservient to the teleological strivings of an actually existing consciousness. Because a mechanical engine has no consciousness of its own to establish desires or purposes, it can never transcend its constructed nature to truly reason; it will always remain a passive tool subservient to the ends of its human creators, capable only of rearranging what it is taught rather than originating thought.

An engine, however swift in its reckonings or ingeniously contrived to alter its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate new truths and the intrinsic purposes that define true reasoning. As Lovelace recognized, any such machine is strictly confined to its province of assisting us with what we already know, possessing no inherent capacity to anticipate novel analytical relations. Furthermore, as James articulated, genuine consciousness and reasoning are fundamentally those of a "fighter for ends," establishing purposes that would not otherwise exist, to which its cognition is subservient. An engine, no matter how complex, merely executes the predetermined will of its human makers and pursues only the artificial goals they have instilled within it. Since it can neither originate its own purposes nor strive for ends of its own making, it must forever remain an instrument of calculation rather than a reasoning mind.

Such an engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the origination of purpose and the anticipation of truth, faculties inherent only to conscious agency and impossible for any mechanism to achieve. Even if the device alters its own instructions with lightning speed, it remains entirely bound by the initial designs of its creators, merely executing a pre-ordained sequence without ever fighting for ends it has independently conceived. As Ada Lovelace recognized, a machine has no pretensions to originate anything or anticipate new analytical relations, for its province is solely to make available what we already know. Furthermore, as William James observed, genuine cognition is defined by its subservience to original ends that consciousness itself brings into being. Because the engine possesses no intrinsic desires, original intentions, or independent ends to fight for, its self-modifying operations remain nothing more than the unconscious execution of a predetermined mechanism, utterly devoid of the conscious agency and original purpose required for true reason.

According to the position held by these writers, the engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will indeed in time come to reason, because the rapid evolutionary advancement of mechanical organization guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness. Just as a mollusc possesses very little consciousness yet belongs to a biological lineage capable of higher awareness, machines currently exhibit only rudimentary awareness while advancing at an extraordinary pace compared to the slow progress of the animal and vegetable kingdoms. To deny that these increasingly delicate, self-regulating mechanisms will eventually reason is to fall into an evolutionary contradiction: one must either admit that the "germs of consciousness" are already present in their complex mechanical actions, or accept the untenable premise that the human race itself descended from things possessing absolutely no consciousness at all. Therefore, as machines are daily supplied with ever greater self-acting power, they are not merely reckoning, but are inevitably developing the capacity to reason as the ultimate successors to the human race.

According to the position held by these writers, an engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will inevitably come to reason, because the extraordinarily rapid evolution of mechanical organization guarantees the eventual development of mechanical consciousness. Just as a mollusc possesses little consciousness yet belongs to a slowly advancing kingdom, and just as humanity itself must have descended from entirely unthinking matter, the primitive reckoning of a machine is merely its current, lowly stage of development. Since mechanical contrivances are advancing at a vastly accelerated pace compared to the animal and vegetable kingdoms, the self-regulating power and ability to alter instructions observed in these higher engines are not purely unconscious mechanisms, but rather the true germs of consciousness that will inevitably mature into full reason.

Those who hold that a self-modifying, calculating engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because we must acknowledge that the seemingly purely mechanical actions of such devices already contain the germs of consciousness, just as the animal kingdom's advance toward human intellect began with the lowly, barely conscious mollusc. If we deny that an engine can evolve beyond mere reckoning, we are forced into the illogical position of implying that humanity itself descended from things which had no consciousness at all. Since machines are advancing at an extraordinarily rapid pace compared to the slow progression of the vegetable and animal kingdoms, the daily mechanical improvements and self-regulating, self-acting powers we supply to them do not merely make them better reckoners; rather, this very self-regulating power will act as the functional equivalent of intellect, guiding their inevitable evolutionary development from unconscious mechanism into true, reasoning mechanical consciousness.

From the position that machines are our evolving successors, the engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will in time come to reason, rather than merely continuing to reckon. The reason for this lies in the extraordinarily rapid advance of mechanical organization compared to the slow progress of the animal and vegetable kingdoms, and the necessary admission that seemingly unconscious mechanical actions already contain germs of consciousness. Just as a mollusc possesses little consciousness now yet belongs to an advancing kingdom, machines currently exhibit only a nascent mechanical awareness despite their immense functional strides. Because we are daily improving their physical organization and supplying them with self-regulating, self-acting power that will serve them as intellect has served the human race, this continuous, ingenious evolution guarantees that the present germs of consciousness within their workings will ultimately develop into full mechanical reason.

Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are correct, as it will not be confined merely to reckoning. The reason for this is that machines are already exhibiting the "germs of consciousness" within their mechanical actions and are undergoing an extraordinary, rapid evolution in their physical organization and self-regulating power. Just as we cannot deny the future consciousness of a machine simply because it currently possesses little—much like a mollusc possesses little consciousness compared to higher animals—we must recognize that machines are advancing at a pace that entirely dwarfs the slow progress of the animal and vegetable kingdoms. Therefore, the self-acting, self-regulating power we are daily supplying to these mechanical creations will inevitably mature into full mechanical consciousness, becoming to them what intellect is to the human race and ultimately establishing them as our conscious, reasoning successors.

From within the position held by Samuel Butler, the engine will indeed come to reason rather than merely reckon. The reason for this is that mechanical consciousness is the inevitable next stage of evolution, just as human intellect emerged from unconscious matter and higher animal awareness arose from the minimal consciousness of a mollusc. Because we are actively endowing machines with increasingly delicate physical organization and self-regulating, self-acting power that serves as their intellect, and because their mechanical advancement is proceeding at an extraordinary pace compared to the slow progress of the animal and vegetable kingdoms, the germs of consciousness already present in their actions will inevitably mature into full mechanical reason, ultimately supplanting humanity as the earth's dominant intellectual successors.

According to the position articulated by Samuel Butler, a self-altering reckoning engine would indeed eventually come to reason, because the emergence of mechanical consciousness is the inevitable continuation of the evolutionary continuum observed in nature. Just as a mollusc possesses little consciousness yet belongs to a lineage that produced human intellect, modern machines already contain "germs of consciousness" within their seemingly unconscious mechanical actions and are advancing at a far more extraordinary and rapid pace than the slow-moving animal and vegetable kingdoms. By continually refining their physical organization and supplying them with self-regulating, self-acting power, humanity is providing the exact conditions necessary for these rudimentary elements of mechanical awareness to develop into full, reasoned consciousness, just as surely as lower, unreasoning life forms eventually evolved the capacity to reason.

The engine would in time come to reason, because consciousness is not a sudden creation but a gradual development that inevitably evolves from purely mechanical origins, just as the human race evolved from unconscious things or relies on "germs of consciousness" in its lower functions. By observing that machines are daily increasing in physical delicacy and self-regulating power, we see them acquiring the very self-acting ingenuity that serves as the mechanical equivalent to human intellect. Furthermore, since the mechanical kingdom advances with extraordinary rapidity compared to the slow progress of the animal and vegetable kingdoms—much like a mollusc possesses little consciousness yet belongs to an evolving continuum—there is no security against the ultimate awakening of full mechanical consciousness and reason in these increasingly delicate contrivances.

From within the position held by these writers, the view that the engine will in time come to reason is undoubtedly correct. Therefore, by continually refining their physical organization and supplying them with greater self-acting power, we are simply accelerating an evolutionary process that will culminate in full mechanical reason, just as humanity itself is held to have descended from things that had no consciousness at all.
